title: BGP Review — 3 border routers, US/EU/JP
summary: This network is strong at the **policy layer** (route filtering, session design, community handling) and weak at the **control-plane-security layer** (no auth, no prefix limits, no TTL security, no BFD). That asymmetry is the story — someone designed the data-plane policy carefully, and the control-plane hardening was deferred. The next review should focus on op-sec posture rather than re-litigating policy.

blocks.claim: The /32 prefix-list pattern in BLOCK_INT_ROUTES is correct and deliberate
blocks.evidence: Many engineers use /24 supernets here and silently break the filter, because BGP prefix-list matching is exact-length unless `le`/`ge` is added. The bug log (`docs/bugs/BGP-001`) shows you debugged this; the current config bakes in the fix.

references.why: RFC 7454 §6 — prefix-list construction at AS boundaries

blocks.claim: iBGP-on-Loopback0 + eBGP-on-physical-/30s is the right asymmetry
blocks.evidence: Loopback iBGP survives any single physical-link failure inside the region; physical eBGP gives fast failure semantics on the inter-region link (link down = session down). Mixing the two would lose one property.

references.why: Cisco design guide on iBGP next-hop and source

blocks.claim: send-community standard large on eBGP — extended deliberately excluded
blocks.evidence: Extended communities are intra-AS metadata (route-targets, encapsulation types) and shouldn't cross AS boundaries. Stripping them at the edge is best practice; your config does this implicitly because send-community simply doesn't include them.

sections.title: Themed gaps
sections.body: The four control-plane gaps share a single root cause: the network is modeled as if the BGP peering subnets are trusted, when control-plane hardening assumes they aren't. The fix is *raise the trust boundary one layer* — once you assume the peering subnet is hostile, all four follow naturally.

blocks.theme: Control-plane trust boundary is too generous
blocks.posture-decision: Treat the peering subnets as untrusted. That single decision motivates every gap below — they're not independent items.

gaps.name: No BGP authentication (MD5/TCP-AO)
gaps.defense: An attacker who reaches the peering subnet inserting routes via spoofed TCP. On cloud or shared transport the /30 is often more reachable than the operator thinks.

gaps.name: No maximum-prefix limits
gaps.defense: A *legitimate* but misbehaving peer (route leak, full-table dump). Cloudflare's 2019 Verizon/CenturyLink incident was caused by exactly this absence.

gaps.name: No TTL security (GTSM)
gaps.defense: Off-path attackers — anyone who can route a packet to your eBGP IP but isn't directly connected. Single-hop eBGP packets always have TTL=255; GTSM rejects anything else.

gaps.name: No BFD
gaps.defense: The 90–180s convergence gap during failures. BGP can't detect link death faster than its hold timer; BFD can in ~1 second.

sections.title: Strengths to preserve
sections.body:
- BLOCK_INT_ROUTES filter — don't refactor; the explicit /32 enumeration is what makes it work.
- Loopback-based iBGP / physical eBGP split — keep this asymmetry.
- next-hop-self on iBGP — required for your topology, don't remove.
- send-community pattern — correctly scopes ext-communities to inside the AS.

sections.title: Action plan
sections.body: Phased remediation. Phase 1 is additive, no maintenance window required.

blocks.name: Security baseline (same-day, no maintenance window)
blocks.goal: Close the four control-plane-security gaps that share the trust-boundary root cause.
blocks.gating-criteria: All BGP sessions remain Established after each push; no traffic-affecting reconfig.

blocks.title: Add BGP authentication on every neighbor
blocks.rollback: `no neighbor X password` on each device
blocks.validate:
python3 "$CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT/skills/forward-device-intel/scripts/get_bgp_peers.py" --device us-border-1 | grep 10.0.0.6
# expected: state=Established, no flap

references.why: prefer TCP-AO over MD5 where supported

blocks.title: Add maximum-prefix limits per neighbor
blocks.config:
router bgp 65000.100
 neighbor 10.0.0.6 maximum-routes 100 75   ! tear down at 100, warn at 75%
 neighbor <ibgp-peer> maximum-routes 10 warning-only
blocks.validate: forward-nqe-query — confirm BGP RIB size <= configured cap on each neighbor

blocks.title: Enable TTL security on directly-connected eBGP
blocks.prereq: P1.1 done (so the BGP renegotiation that follows happens inside an authenticated session)
blocks.config:
router bgp 65000.100
 neighbor 10.0.0.6 ttl maximum-hops 1
 neighbor 10.0.0.18 ttl maximum-hops 1
blocks.validate: BGP session re-establishes within ~30s; new packets carry TTL=255

blocks.title: Enable BFD on eBGP links
blocks.prereq: P1 complete; both ends of each link must support compatible BFD timers
blocks.config:
router bgp 65000.100
 neighbor 10.0.0.6 bfd
 neighbor 10.0.0.6 bfd interval 300 min-rx 300 multiplier 3
blocks.validate: Force a link down; convergence < 1s observable in path-analysis

sections.title: Appendix
sections.body: Per-finding detail (config, validation, full references). Preserved here for compliance / verification. Most readers will not need this.

blocks.body:
F-001 No BGP authentication — see P1.1.
F-002 No maximum-prefix limits — see P1.2.
F-003 No TTL security — see P1.3.
F-004 No BFD — see P2.1.
F-005 No log-neighbor-changes — Phase 3.
F-006 No graceful-restart helper — Phase 3.
F-007 No soft-reconfiguration inbound — Phase 3.
